g.data-narration: Election Algorithms in Distributed Systems - Learning about coordinator selection for fault tolerance and system coordination in distributed computing environments with multiple processes.
g.text: Election Algorithms

g.data-narration: Election algorithms select a coordinator from multiple processes in distributed systems. They ensure fault tolerance when the current coordinator fails or during system startup.
g.text: What are Election Algorithms?
Election algorithms select a coordinator/leader from a group of processes in distributed systems.
The coordinator handles resource management, synchronization, and maintaining consistency.
Essential when coordinator fails (crash, network partition) or during initial system boot-up.

g.data-narration: The Bully Algorithm uses process IDs where the highest ID becomes coordinator. When a process detects failure, it sends election messages to all higher-ID processes.
g.text: Bully Algorithm (Highest ID Wins)
• Process with highest ID becomes coordinator
• When P1 detects failure: sends ELECTION to P2,P3,P4,P5
• Higher processes respond OK; highest responder becomes coordinator
• Message complexity: O(n²) worst case, O(n) best case

g.data-narration: The Ring Algorithm organizes processes in a logical circle. Election messages circulate sequentially, with each process adding its ID until the message returns to the initiator.
g.text: Ring Algorithm (Logical Circle)
• Processes arranged logically: P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5 → P1
• P1 creates election [1], passes to P2 which adds [2], continues to P5
• Message returns to P1 with full list [1,2,3,4]; highest ID (P4) wins
• Message complexity: O(n) in all cases

g.data-narration: Bully Algorithm is simple but message-intensive with O(n²) worst case. Ring Algorithm is more efficient with O(n) messages but slower due to sequential propagation.
g.text: Comparison: Bully vs Ring
Bully Algorithm
• Simple to implement
• O(n²) worst, O(n) best
Ring Algorithm
• Requires ring maintenance
• O(n) messages always

g.data-narration: Election algorithms assume unique process IDs, reliable channels, and crash failures. Safety ensures only one coordinator; liveness ensures eventual election.
g.text: Assumptions & Correctness Properties
• Assumptions: Unique process IDs, reliable channels, crash failures (not Byzantine)
• Safety: At most ONE coordinator at any time (prevents split-brain scenarios)
• Liveness: Eventually a coordinator IS elected (if system stabilizes and ≥1 process alive)
• Processes detect failure via timeout (no heartbeat messages)

g.data-narration: Real-world applications include Apache Zookeeper, etcd, and consensus protocols like Raft and Paxos. These power distributed databases, cloud platforms, and blockchain networks.
g.text: Real-World Applications
• Zookeeper, etcd for service coordination • Raft/Paxos in distributed databases
• Cloud platforms (AWS, Azure) for HA • Blockchain PoS for block production
